summary: Jeff Atwood explores the counterintuitive value of failure in professional development, drawing on Will Wright's hiring philosophy of preferring candidates with more failures on their resume. He connects this to Edison's famous reframing of failed experiments as discoveries, and a sociologist's research showing that the best surgeons are those who readily acknowledge and learn from their mistakes. The post argues that in software development, where project failure rates are high, the ability to honestly assess and learn from failures is more valuable than a track record of unexamined successes.
key_points: Will Wright actively seeks to hire people who have experienced more failures, viewing it as the best learning system
The difference between successful and unsuccessful surgeons lies not in technical skill but in their attitude toward failure and willingness to acknowledge mistakes
Asking candidates about their worst mistakes is a powerful interview technique - those who claim no mistakes are the worst candidates
Software development's high failure rate means professionals should expect to fail regularly and treat each failure as a learning opportunity
Refusing to acknowledge failures means cheating yourself out of the best professional development opportunities
money_quotes: I'm actually more likely to hire someone based on how many failures they've experienced. I think it's the best learning system.
I have discovered a thousand things that don't work.

Far more than technical skills or intelligence, what was necessary for success was the sort of attitude that Quest has – a practical-minded obsession with the possibility and the consequences of failure.
The people who said, 'Gee, I haven't really had one,' or, 'I've had a couple of bad outcomes but they were due to things outside my control' – invariably those were the worst candidates.
They had the ability to rethink everything that they'd done and imagine how they might have done it differently.
You should always be failing some of the time, and learning from those failures in an honest way.
key_insight: The willingness to honestly acknowledge and learn from failures is a better predictor of professional success than a spotless track record, because those who claim no mistakes lack the self-awareness necessary for growth.
